items.md:
from the days when Donald was fleeing from the Needlework Club.
But, if anything, the gap between Barks's stories and the run of comic-book stories widened in the fifties, since the quality of most comic books fell much more rapidly than the quality of *Walt Disney's Comics* and *Uncle Scrooge*. Barks's stories could be labeled inferior only by comparison with his earlier work. In fact, Western leaned on him more and more throughout the fifties, to the point that in 1960, he illustrated three hundred and fifty-eight pages (including more than a hundred pages that were written by others) and fifteen covers.
Comic-book quality declined in the fifties in large part because of the ferocious assualts on the comics by those who found in them the roots of social evils. Dell, the company that published most of the comic books that Western produced, was less affected by these attacks than any other major publisher, because Western's comics were already so "clean," and Dell refused to join the Comics Code Authority because the CCA's standards were supposedly too lax. As a substitute for the CCA's seal of purity, Dell ran an unctuous "Pledge to Parents" in every issue, starting in 1955, along with the slogan "Dell Comics Are Good Comics." Western's editors did their part by ridding the comic books of anything that might give offense, to the point that "Dell" became synonymous with "dull."
Barks did not get lost in the growing thicket of taboos very often. At least three stories were shelved in the fifties, and others were changed (in some cases for understandable reasons), but Barks's percentage of acceptance remained remarkably high. His editors, if they did not help him, did not get much in his way — at least not directly.
Barks's censorship was mostly of the self-imposed variety. He must have been aware of the changing climate, and he changed his stories accordingly. Some of his earlier stories — "The Old Castle's Secret" with its grave-
yards and skeletons, for example, and "Voodoo Hoodoo" with its racial caricatures and jungle magic — surely would have been rejected if they had been offered to Western fresh in the middle fifties.
Although he sensed the new restraints, and worked within them, Barks unquestionably found them frustrating. In the February 1955 issue of *Walt Disney's Comics*, he pitted the nephews and Donald directly against one another, for the first time in years. He did so again in the March 1956 *Walt Disney's Comics*, reworking the Donald Duck story in the June 1944 issue. In June 1956, when yet another such story was on sale in the July issue, one of his editors sent him "a letter from a mother, which we feel is a fair criticism." This mother had evidently decided that the March 1956 story had bruised her son's tender sensibilities. Barks's reply fairly steamed on the page:
"Very well, I will avoid any more stories using conflict or rivalry between Don and the kids. ...
"I have bought a number of Tom and Jerry's, Porky Pigs, Chester Chipmunks, etc., and will confine plots and gag situations to 'switches' on the material I find in those safe family journals.
"Seriously, I think the woman who wrote this letter and sent the marked pages of the comics is a neurotic and so is her sniffling son. ...
"When I worked in the Disney story department in the duck unit, the basic theme in a great number of the Donald Duck shorts was rivalry between the kids and Donald. Certainly the main body of the public didn't object to this theme, for during those years, Donald and his nephews overtook and passed Mickey Mouse and his nephews in box office popularity.
"After starting to work for Whitman I kept to the proven story lines as much as possible. The 10 page Donalds through the first several years were liberally sprinkled with rivalry
